confidence.disclosure: Ali is present in almost every major event of the sirah and the early caliphate, and his biography is one of the most extensively written in Islamic history — though Sunni and Shi'a traditions sometimes weight events differently. The First Fitna battles (Camel, Siffin, Nahrawan) are attested with unusual geographic precision, including his movement between Kufa and the battlefields. His assassination on 28 January 661 is the only 'day'-precision entry in his journey — the date is agreed across sources.

journey.event: Born to Abu Talib ibn Abd al-Muttalib and Fatimah bint Asad. A strong tradition, unique in Islamic history, holds that his mother entered the Ka'bah and gave birth to him inside the sacred house. He was named Ali by his parents and was the Prophet Muhammad's first cousin.

Raised in the household of the Prophet Muhammad after Abu Talib's financial difficulties during a famine made it difficult to support his large family. The Prophet took Ali into his care as a gesture of support to his uncle, and Ali grew up under his direct guidance and companionship.

Among the very first to accept Islam, widely regarded as the first male youth to embrace the faith. He witnessed the Prophet and Khadijah praying together, asked about their practice, and accepted the message without hesitation, joining them in worship at a time when Islam was still a secret.

Endured the years of Quraysh persecution alongside the Prophet and the early Muslim community. This included the three-year boycott in the Valley of Abu Talib, during which the Banu Hashim were cut off from trade and social contact. Ali remained a steadfast supporter throughout these trials.

On the night of the Hijra (Laylat al-Mabit), Ali slept in the Prophet's bed wrapped in his green cloak to deceive the Quraysh assassins who had surrounded the house. He risked his life to ensure the Prophet's safe departure, and remained in Mecca for several days afterward to return the goods entrusted to the Prophet by the Meccans.

Migrated to Medina on foot a few days after the Prophet's departure, having completed the task of returning all entrusted possessions. He arrived with swollen and bleeding feet and joined the Prophet at Quba before entering Medina together with the nascent Muslim community.

Married Fatimah bint Muhammad, the Prophet's youngest daughter, in a simple ceremony. The marriage was deeply significant: it united the Prophet's bloodline with that of his closest male relative, and from this union came Hasan and Husayn, through whom the Prophet's lineage would continue.

Fought with distinction at the Battle of Badr, the first major military engagement of Islam. Ali served as one of the three Muslim champions in the opening duels, killing Walid ibn Utba, and continued fighting throughout the battle, killing several Quraysh warriors and carrying the standard of the Muhajirun.

Defended the Prophet at the Battle of Uhud when the Muslim lines broke after the archers abandoned their positions. Ali was among the small group of companions who stood firm around the wounded Prophet, shielding him from the Quraysh counterattack and sustaining multiple wounds himself.

During the Battle of the Trench, Ali fought the legendary Quraysh champion Amr ibn Abd Wudd in single combat when the veteran warrior crossed the trench and challenged the Muslims. Ali's victory over Amr is one of the most celebrated duels in Islamic military history and was a decisive moment in breaking the siege.

Served as the scribe who wrote the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah on the Prophet's dictation. When the Quraysh envoy Suhayl ibn Amr objected to the phrase 'Muhammad, the Messenger of God,' the Prophet instructed Ali to erase it; Ali famously refused out of reverence, so the Prophet erased the words himself.

Led the final assault on the fortress of Khaybar after previous attempts by other commanders had failed. The Prophet announced he would give the banner to one whom God and His Messenger love, and handed it to Ali. He breached the fortress gate, earning the title Asadullah (Lion of God).

Participated in the Conquest of Mecca as the Muslim army entered the city with minimal resistance. Ali was entrusted with helping to cleanse the Ka'bah of its idols, climbing on the Prophet's shoulders to pull down the largest idol from atop the sacred house, marking the restoration of monotheistic worship.

Fought at the Battle of Hunayn when the Hawazin and Thaqif tribes ambushed the Muslim army in a narrow valley. Ali was among the handful of companions who held firm around the Prophet when the main force fled in the initial chaos, helping to rally the troops for the eventual Muslim victory.

Sent by the Prophet to Yemen as a judge, governor, and envoy to spread Islam among the southern tribes. Ali adjudicated disputes, collected revenues, and established Muslim governance in the region. His mission to Yemen was one of the most significant administrative appointments of the Prophetic era.

Joined the Prophet for the Farewell Pilgrimage (Hajjat al-Wada), the only Hajj the Prophet performed. Ali arrived from Yemen to meet the Prophet at Mecca. This pilgrimage, attended by over a hundred thousand Muslims, was the last great gathering of the community before the Prophet's death.

Fatimah bint Muhammad, Ali's beloved wife and the Prophet's daughter, died a few months after her father. Her death was a profound personal loss and also carried political significance, as it removed the most direct familial link between Ali and the Prophet's legacy in the eyes of the community.

Served in an advisory capacity during the caliphate of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. Although the question of succession at Saqifah created tension, Ali eventually pledged allegiance and continued to serve the community as a trusted counselor on matters of Quranic interpretation, jurisprudence, and governance.

Remained in Medina throughout the caliphate of Umar ibn al-Khattab, serving as a senior advisor, judge, and scholar. Umar frequently consulted Ali on complex legal and theological questions, and is reported to have said, 'Were it not for Ali, Umar would have perished,' acknowledging his indispensable counsel.

Served as one of six members of the shura council appointed by Umar before his death to select the next caliph. The council ultimately chose Uthman ibn Affan after Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf cast the deciding vote. Ali accepted the outcome and continued to serve the community.

Lived in Medina during the caliphate of Uthman ibn Affan, continuing his role as a respected scholar and advisor. As discontent grew against Uthman's policies, Ali attempted to mediate between the caliph and the rebels, urging restraint on both sides, but was unable to prevent the crisis that culminated in Uthman's assassination.

Elected as the fourth Rashidun caliph following the assassination of Uthman. Ali accepted the caliphate reluctantly after receiving the pledge of allegiance at the Prophet's Mosque, though several prominent companions withheld their support. His accession marked the beginning of the turbulent First Fitna.

Transferred the capital of the caliphate from Medina to Kufa in Iraq, a strategic decision that placed the seat of government closer to the empire's center of gravity and its military garrisons. This move permanently ended Medina's role as the political capital of the Muslim world.

Fought the Battle of the Camel (Jamal) against a coalition led by Aisha, Talha ibn Ubaydallah, and Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, who demanded justice for Uthman's murder. The battle, named for Aisha's camel around which fighting raged, ended in Ali's victory. Talha and Zubayr were killed, and Aisha was sent back to Medina with honor.

Marshaled his Iraqi forces and marched north from Kufa along the Euphrates to confront Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan, the governor of Syria who refused to recognize Ali's caliphate. The campaign involved weeks of preparation and movement across the Jazira region toward the Syrian frontier.

Engaged Mu'awiya's Syrian army at the Battle of Siffin on the banks of the Euphrates. After weeks of skirmishes, Ali's forces were on the verge of victory when Mu'awiya's troops raised copies of the Quran on their lances, calling for arbitration. Under pressure from his own ranks, Ali reluctantly agreed to halt the fighting.

The arbitration at Adhruh (also associated with Dumat al-Jandal) between Ali's representative Abu Musa al-Ash'ari and Mu'awiya's representative Amr ibn al-As ended inconclusively and politically damaged Ali's position. The failure of arbitration deepened divisions and emboldened both the Kharijites and Mu'awiya's supporters.

Fought the Battle of Nahrawan against the Kharijites, former supporters who rejected the Siffin arbitration and declared both Ali and Mu'awiya to be unbelievers. Ali first offered amnesty, which many accepted, then decisively defeated the remaining rebels. The battle eliminated the Kharijite military threat but deepened disillusionment among his Iraqi base.

Returned to Kufa and attempted to consolidate his administration and raise new forces for a campaign against Mu'awiya. However, the Kufan troops were exhausted and demoralized by continuous warfare, and many refused to answer his call to march, leaving Ali increasingly isolated as Mu'awiya extended his control over Egypt and the Hijaz.

Spent his final years governing from Kufa, delivering the sermons and letters later compiled in Nahj al-Balagha. Despite his unrivaled learning and piety, his political authority steadily eroded as Mu'awiya consolidated power across the provinces. Ali continued to adjudicate disputes and teach, remaining devoted to justice until the end.

Struck by a poison-coated sword wielded by the Kharijite Abd al-Rahman ibn Muljam while leading the Fajr prayer at the Great Mosque of Kufa on 19 Ramadan 40 AH (approximately 28 January 661 CE). Ali died two days later from his wounds. His burial place was kept secret; the shrine at Najaf is the traditional site. His death ended the Rashidun caliphate.
